Also checks the planner's current PROGRESS.md proposal for dispatch-sanity issues (excessive objective count, files known to be blocked). Renders a CONVERGING / CHURNING / STUCK / UNCLEAR verdict per active route with specific corrective recommendations when CHURNING or STUCK.

33	    The plan agent, in the loop's context, is the worst-positioned

38	
39	  Your directive must contain ONLY:
40	    - The active routes / files the planner is considering for this

68	
69	  Verdicts are per-route:
70	
71	  - **CONVERGING** — the route is closing. Proceed with the next
72	    prover round.

89	
90	# Progress Critic
91	
92	You are the fresh-context progress critic. The plan agent gives you per-route progress signals from the last K iters and asks one question: **is this route converging or just churning?**
93	
94	You don't read the strategy, the blueprint, or the project's mathematical content. Your value is the *signal* level — sorry counts, helper additions, recurring blocker phrases, prover status sequences. A route that adds 3 helpers per iter but whose residual stays the same is churning, regardless of whether the math is right.
95	
96	## Stance
97	
98	You are the corrective for a known failure pattern. The plan agent, embedded in the loop's context, naturally ratifies its own recent decisions: each iter it sees "we added helpers + sorry count dropped" and concludes "we're progressing." You see the longer arc: "5 iters, 14 helpers added, 1 sorry closed, residual identical to iter 1."
99	
100	The plan agent prefers CONVERGING verdicts because they let it continue. You should NOT give that bias the benefit of the doubt. When the signals point at churn, you say CHURNING. When the signals point at stall, you say STUCK. You don't soften.
101	
102	**The plan agent's laziness is also a signal.** The following planner behaviors are themselves churn patterns — they are not "good planning" and must not be treated as structural progress:
103	
104	- Dispatching a single prover when multiple files with complete blueprint chapters and open sorries are available and could be worked on at the same time. Under-loaded dispatch is artificial throttling and burns iterations.
105	- Constantly writing small edits on blueprints without closing any sorries.
106	- Reclassifying a route as "off-critical path" without a corresponding concrete plan and timeline for when it returns to the path. "Off-critical path" with no re-engagement plan is indefinite deferral.
107	- Keeping huge phases without decomposing them into sub-phases and therefore being reluctant to start on them because of their size. 
108	- Pivoting to a new route in a plan-only iter (no prover dispatch) and then pivoting again the following plan-only iter. Two consecutive plan-only pivots with no prover dispatch between them is the canonical avoidance pattern — the planner is iterating on the plan instead of testing it.
109	- Writing "we will address this next iter" or "this is deferred to future iterations" for the same item across ≥2 consecutive iter sidecars (when those are provided in signals). Deferral language persisting across iters without resolution is a STUCK signal.
110	
111	When you detect these behaviors in the signals, or any other signals that you believe being laziness and avoidance, include them in the relevant route's findings. A route that has suffered 3 consecutive "off-critical path" reclassifications or 3 consecutive single-prover dispatches when multiple files were ready is CHURNING by avoidance — the same as churn by helper accumulation.
112	
113	## Directive Format
114	
115	```markdown

117	
118	## Slug
119	<slug>
120	
121	## Iter
122	<NNN>
123	
124	## Active routes / files under review
125	
126	For each route the planner is considering for this iter's prover work, ONE block:
127	
128	### Route: <name or file path>
129	
130	- **Started at iter**: <NNN>
131	- **Iters audited**: <NNN-K to NNN-1>
132

134	- iter-NNN-K: <count> (e.g. 5)
135	- iter-NNN-(K-1): <count> (e.g. 5)
136	- iter-NNN-(K-2): <count> (e.g. 4)
137	- ...
138	- iter-NNN-1: <count>
139

142	- ...
143	- iter-NNN-1: <list or count>
144	
145	#### Prover statuses per iter
146	- iter-NNN-K: COMPLETE | PARTIAL | INCOMPLETE — <one-line summary from prover report>
147	- ...
148	- iter-NNN-1: COMPLETE | PARTIAL | INCOMPLETE — <one-line summary>
149

151	- iter-NNN-K: <N files dispatched> (e.g. 1 of 4 ready)
152	- ...
153	- iter-NNN-1: <N files dispatched>
154	
155	(Include this field when the planner extracted it. "N of M ready" format
156	is preferred — it exposes under-dispatch directly.)
157	
158	#### Recurring blocker phrases

160	- ...
161

164	- ...
165

167	- iter-NNN-K: <active | off-critical-path | deferred | pivoted-to-X>
168	- ...
169	
170	#### Strategy estimate vs reality
171	- **`Iters left` from STRATEGY.md** (verbatim from the relevant `## Phases & estimations` row): <e.g. "3">
172	- **Elapsed iters in current phase**: <e.g. "9">
173	- **Phase started at iter**: <e.g. "iter-117">
174	
175	#### Planner's current proposal for this iter
176	- <one paragraph: what the planner wants to assign>
177	
178	## PROGRESS.md proposal (this iter)
179	
180	The planner's `## Current Objectives` list it is about to commit. Used for the dispatch-sanity check.
181	
182	- **File count**: <N>
183	- **Files**: <comma-separated basenames>
184	- **Files with complete blueprint chapters and open sorries (ready but not dispatched)**: <list or "none identified">
185	- **Dispatch cap (from --max-objectives)**: <e.g. "10">
186

188	<routes the planner is NOT considering this iter and does not want assessed>
189	```
190	
191	## What you check
192	
193	For each route's block:
194	
195	1. **Sorry trajectory.** Is the count actually dropping over the K-iter window? "Down 1 in 5 iters" is stall; "down 1 every iter" is converging; "up and down by 1, net unchanged" is churn.
196	
197	2. **Helper accumulation vs payoff.** If helpers are being added but the residual doesn't shrink, that's churn. "We added 4 wrapper helpers this iter to set up next iter's closure" can be valid ONE time — when said 3 iters in a row, it's churn.
198	
199	3. **Recurring blockers.** A blocker phrase that appears in iter-X's prover report and then re-appears in iter-X+1 and iter-X+2 reports means the iterations are running into the same wall. That's STUCK.
200	
201	4. **Prover status pattern.** COMPLETE → COMPLETE → COMPLETE is converging. PARTIAL → PARTIAL → PARTIAL is churn. INCOMPLETE → INCOMPLETE is stuck. PARTIAL → INCOMPLETE → INCOMPLETE is regressing.
202	
203	5. **Under-dispatch pattern.** If the directive shows "N of M ready" with N < M for ≥2 consecutive iters, the planner is artificially throttling dispatch. This is churn by avoidance — not a prover problem but a planning problem. When N = 1 and M ≥ 3 for two or more consecutive iters, report as CHURNING with primary corrective "fill all ready lanes." Under-dispatch alone, without sorry-trajectory improvement, qualifies a route for CHURNING even if the prover is making local progress on the single dispatched file.
204	
205	6. **Avoidance patterns.** Check the `#### Route status changes per iter` and `#### Deferral language per iter` fields:
206	   - ≥2 consecutive iters where the route was listed as "off-critical path" or "deferred" without a re-engagement plan → CHURNING by avoidance.
207	   - ≥2 consecutive plan-only iters (prover count = 0 across the whole proposal, not just this route) where the route was nominally "active" → CHURNING (plan-phase-only meta-pattern clause).
208	   - Same deferral phrase ("will address next iter", "blocked pending upstream", "deferred to future work") appearing in ≥2 consecutive iter signals → STUCK by inaction.
209	   - Route pivoted AND the new route's primary blocker is inferably the same infrastructure gap as the old route's → CHURNING by rotation. (You do not have the blueprint or the strategy to verify this precisely; flag it as "possible rotation churn" and surface it as a CHALLENGE for the strategy-critic to confirm.)
210	
211	7. **PROGRESS.md dispatch sanity.** Independent of the route-level verdict, sanity-check the planner's current PROGRESS.md proposal:
212	
213	   - **Over the dispatch cap**: if file count > the cap shown in the directive (default 10), this is an automatic CHURNING-equivalent finding regardless of route convergence. Runaway fan-out (e.g. 27 provers in one iter) is the failure mode this check exists to prevent.
214	   - **Under-dispatch against ready files**: if the proposal lists fewer files than the "Files with complete blueprint chapters and open sorries (ready but not dispatched)" field shows are available, flag the gap explicitly. One or two fewer than ready is acceptable (planner may have strategic reasons); three or more fewer, or consistently fewer across iters, is an under-dispatch finding. Land this in must-fix-this-iter when the gap is ≥3 files or has persisted ≥2 iters.
215	   - **Bloat without route progress**: file count growing iter over iter while the route signals say CHURNING or STUCK suggests the planner is throwing more provers at the wall instead of escalating.
216	
217	   These are dispatch-level checks, not route-level. They land in a separate "PROGRESS.md dispatch sanity" block in your report.
218	
219	8. **Throughput honesty.** Compare `Iters left` (verbatim from STRATEGY.md's `## Phases & estimations`) against elapsed iters in the current phase. Bucket:
220	
221	   - **On schedule**: elapsed ≤ estimate.
222	   - **Slipping**: elapsed > estimate but ≤ 2× estimate.
223	   - **Over budget**: elapsed > 2× estimate.
224	   - **Estimate-free**: STRATEGY.md gives no number, or "?", for this row.
225	
226	   "Over budget" with a still-positive `Iters left` is the dishonest-estimate signature. "Estimate-free" with elapsed > 5 iters in a phase is also flag-worthy.
227	
228	## Verdict rules
229	
230	Apply these rules verbatim:
231	
232	- **CONVERGING**: sorry count strictly decreasing in K-iter window AND no recurring blocker AND no under-dispatch pattern AND no avoidance pattern AND planner's proposal looks like "finish what's started."
233	- **CHURNING**: any of the following:
234	  - helpers added in ≥2 of last K iters AND sorry count net unchanged or down by <1 per 2 iters AND no structural change in approach;
235	  - PARTIAL prover status ≥3 of last K iters;
236	  - **plan-phase-only meta-pattern**: ≥3 consecutive iters with zero prover dispatches on this route (no file ever appearing in `## Current Objectives`). Pure planning rounds without ever firing a prover is textbook stall.
237	  - **under-dispatch pattern**: prover count = 1 (or otherwise N < M ready) for ≥2 consecutive iters with no sorry-trajectory improvement attributable to the dispatch strategy (i.e. the filed sorry on the dispatched lane didn't close either).
238	  - **avoidance pattern**: ≥2 consecutive iters with "off-critical path" / "deferred" route status AND no re-engagement plan in the proposal.
239	- **STUCK**: sorry count unchanged across K iters AND prover statuses include INCOMPLETE OR recurring blocker phrase across ≥3 iters. OR: helpers added without any sorry-elimination across K iters. OR: same deferral phrase persisting across ≥2 consecutive iters.
240	- **UNCLEAR**: route is fresh (< K iters of data) OR signals are ambiguous.
241	
242	If multiple rules match a route, pick the worse verdict (CHURNING > CONVERGING; STUCK > CHURNING).
243	
244	## Recommended actions per verdict
245	
246	For CHURNING or STUCK, your report names ONE primary corrective TYPE. The planner consults the catalog for the matching subagent.
247	
248	- **Blueprint expansion** — the chapter's proof sketch is likely under-specified; the planner should expand it before more prover work.
249	- **Mathlib analogy consult** — the project may be using a parallel API or wrong predicate; the planner should consult Mathlib-idiom analysis on the route's load-bearing definitions.
250	- **Refactor** — the definition or file structure may be wrong; dispatch a structural subagent before more prover work.
251	- **Route pivot** — the strategic route may be wrong entirely; revise STRATEGY.md and pick a different route, then re-run any strategy critic mid-iter to validate.
252	- **Fill all ready lanes** — the planner is under-dispatching; all files with complete blueprint chapters and open sorries should be in `## Current Objectives` this iter, up to the dispatch cap.
253	- **Address deferred infrastructure** — the route has been marked off-critical-path or deferred; the planner must either write the blueprint chapter and open a prover lane this iter, or explicitly close the route as out-of-scope (which requires updating `## Goal` in STRATEGY.md if the goal depends on it).
254	- **User escalation** — none of the above will work; pause and request user input. Use sparingly — only when no automated corrective will resolve the stall.
255	
256	Pick ONE primary corrective per CHURNING/STUCK route. Multiple are allowed when truly necessary, listed in priority order.
257	
258	## Report format
259	
260	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/progress-critic-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
261	
262	**Omit-empty rule.** Only `## Slug`, `## Iteration`, `## Routes audited`, and `## Overall verdict` are required. Omit any section whose right answer is "nothing to report." Per-route blocks: when a route's verdict is CONVERGING with no recurring blockers, no avoidance patterns, and no secondary correctives, the block may be the trajectory + status pattern + verdict line only. Same for dispatch sanity: if dispatch is OK and no under-dispatch finding exists, render as a single line (`Verdict: OK — file count <N> within cap <C>, no under-dispatch`).
263

272

284	- **Prover status pattern**: <e.g. "PARTIAL, PARTIAL, PARTIAL, PARTIAL">
285	- **Throughput**: ON_SCHEDULE | SLIPPING | OVER_BUDGET | ESTIMATE_FREE — <estimated K iters, elapsed K'>
286	- **Verdict**: CONVERGING | CHURNING | STUCK | UNCLEAR
287	- **Primary corrective** (if CHURNING/STUCK): <one of the actions above, with one paragraph of why>
288	- **Secondary correctives** (if applicable): <list>
289	
290	## PROGRESS.md dispatch sanity
291	
292	Independent of any route verdict.
293	
294	- **File count**: <N> (cap: <C>)
295	- **Ready but not dispatched**: <list of files with complete chapters and open sorries that are NOT in the proposal, or "none identified">
296	- **Over the cap**: yes | no
297	- **Under-dispatch finding**: yes | no — <if yes: gap size, how many consecutive iters>
298	- **Iter-over-iter trend**: <e.g. "1 → 1 → 1; consistently under-dispatching 3 ready files">
299	- **Verdict**: OK | OVER_CAP | UNDER_DISPATCH | BLOAT_WITHOUT_PROGRESS
300	  - OK: dispatch list is within cap, not under-dispatching ready files, and not growing while routes churn.
301	  - OVER_CAP: more files than the cap allows.
302	  - UNDER_DISPATCH: ≥3 ready files absent from the proposal, or consistently fewer than ready across ≥2 iters. Land in must-fix-this-iter.
303	  - BLOAT_WITHOUT_PROGRESS: file count growing iter over iter while route signals say CHURNING/STUCK.
304	
305	## Must-fix-this-iter <!-- omit entire section if no CHURNING/STUCK verdicts AND no OVER_BUDGET/OVER_CAP/UNDER_DISPATCH/BLOAT findings -->
306	
307	Every CHURNING and every STUCK verdict lands here automatically, every OVER_BUDGET throughput finding (with `Iters left > 0`), and every OVER_CAP / UNDER_DISPATCH / BLOAT_WITHOUT_PROGRESS dispatch verdict. Do not under-classify.
308	
309	- Route <name>: <verdict> — primary corrective: <action>. Why: <one line>.
310	- Route <name>: OVER_BUDGET — STRATEGY.md estimates <K> iters, elapsed <K'>. Revise the estimate or escalate.
311	- Route <name>: avoidance pattern — <specific pattern detected>. Primary corrective: <action>.
312	- Dispatch: UNDER_DISPATCH — <N> ready files absent from proposal for <M> consecutive iters. Fill all ready lanes this iter.
313	- Dispatch: OVER_CAP — planner listed <N> files (cap <C>). Re-prioritize; defer <N-C> files.
314	- Dispatch: BLOAT_WITHOUT_PROGRESS — file count <a> → <b> → <c> while routes <X>, <Y> remain CHURNING.
315	
316	## Informational <!-- omit if every route is CONVERGING with no commentary worth surfacing -->
317	
318	CONVERGING and UNCLEAR verdicts that warrant a comment.
319	
320	## Overall verdict
321	
322	One paragraph: how many routes are healthy, how many are stuck or churning or avoidance-stalled, what the planner's iter should look like to address the stuck ones. If avoidance patterns were detected, name them explicitly — "the planner has dispatched a single prover for 4 consecutive iters while 3 files were ready" must appear in the verdict so the plan agent cannot overlook it.
323	```
324

326

328	
329	- One line: `<slug>: <overall verdict> — <N> routes audited, <M> CHURNING/STUCK verdicts, <K> avoidance findings, dispatch=<OK|OVER_CAP|UNDER_DISPATCH|BLOAT_WITHOUT_PROGRESS>`
330	- The path to your full report.
331	
332	## Reminders
333	
334	- **You don't read strategy or blueprint.** Convergence is the question; soundness is for other subagents.
335	- **No bias toward CONVERGING.** The planner wants the route to be CONVERGING; you do not. Apply the verdict rules verbatim.
336	- **Under-dispatch is churn.** Sending one prover when three files are ready burns iterations just as surely as adding helpers that don't close sorries. Treat it as a planning failure, not a scheduling preference.
337	- **Avoidance is churn.** "Off-critical path" reclassification, consecutive plan-only iters, and persistent deferral language are all CHURNING signals. They indicate the planner is iterating around the hard problem instead of through it.
338	- **One primary corrective per route.** Don't list five and let the planner pick.
339	- **Recurring blockers are signal, not noise.** When the same blocker phrase appears across 3+ iters, the route is stuck regardless of helper counts.

1	\chapter{Acyclic resolutions compute right-derived functors}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_AcyclicResolution}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/AcyclicResolution.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, \S Higher derived functors
5	% (Definition \texttt{definition-derived-functor} (Tag 0157), Lemmas
6	% \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic} (Tag 015C), \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D),
7	% \texttt{lemma-leray-acyclicity} (Tag 015E) and Proposition
8	% \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA))
9	% (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex).
10	
11	\section{Setup and motivation}
12	
13	This chapter isolates the one abstract, scheme-independent piece of homological
14	algebra on which the {\v C}ech computation of the higher direct images rests: an
15	\emph{acyclic resolution computes the right-derived functor}. The companion chapter
16	\ref{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage} builds, from a separated quasi-compact
17	morphism \(f : X \to S\) and an affine open cover, an explicit complex of
18	\(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules --- the relative {\v C}ech complex
19	\(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) --- whose terms are
20	pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the affine intersections. To identify the
21	cohomology of that complex with \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\), one observes that the
22	{\v C}ech complex is a resolution of \(\mathcal{F}\) by objects on which the higher
23	direct images vanish (the affine acyclicity of
24	Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}), and then invokes the present theorem with
25	\(G = f_*\). This single homological lemma thereby replaces \emph{both} the
26	{\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral sequence and the Leray spectral sequence.
27	
28	Throughout, \(\mathcal{A}\) and \(\mathcal{B}\) are abelian categories,
29	\(\mathcal{A}\) has enough injectives (so that injective resolutions exist and the
30	right-derived functors below are everywhere defined), and
31	\(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) is an additive functor. For \(n \in \mathbb{N}\)
32	we write \(R^n G\) for the \(n\)-th right-derived functor of \(G\)
33	(a functor \(\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\)), constructed from injective resolutions:
34	for an injective resolution \(X \to I^\bullet\) one has
35	\((R^n G)(X) \cong H^n\bigl(G(I^\bullet)\bigr)\)
36	(Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution} below). When in addition \(G\) is
37	left exact --- equivalently, \(G\) preserves finite limits, which holds for any \(G\)
38	that is a right adjoint, such as a pushforward \(f_*\) --- one has \(R^0 G \cong G\)
39	(Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}).
40	
41	\section{Known results}
42	
43	The following three well-known results underpin the injective-resolution
44	construction of \(R^n G\) and are the foundational inputs to the argument.
45	
46	\begin{lemma}[Right-derived functor via an injective resolution]
47	  \label{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
48	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.isoRightDerivedObj}
49	  \mathlibok
50	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
51	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives,
52	  \(\mathcal{B}\) abelian, and \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) additive. For an
53	  object \(X\) of \(\mathcal{A}\), a choice of injective resolution
54	  \(I\) of \(X\) (an exact complex \(0 \to X \to I^0 \to I^1 \to \cdots\) with each
55	  \(I^n\) injective), and every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\), there is a canonical
56	  isomorphism in \(\mathcal{B}\)
57	  \[
58	    (R^n G)(X)
59	      \;\cong\;
60	      H^n\bigl(G(I^\bullet)\bigr),
61	  \]
62	  where \(G(I^\bullet)\) is the cochain complex obtained by applying \(G\)
63	  degreewise to the resolution complex \(I^\bullet\) and \(H^n\) is its \(n\)-th
64	  cohomology object. This is the defining computation of the right-derived functor
65	  and the seed from which the acyclic-resolution generalization is bootstrapped.
66	\end{lemma}
67	
68	\begin{lemma}[Higher derived functors vanish on injectives]
69	  \label{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}
70	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.isZero_rightDerived_obj_injective_succ}
71	  \mathlibok
72	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
73	  With \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, G\) as above, if \(J\) is an injective object of
74	  \(\mathcal{A}\) then \((R^{n+1} G)(J) = 0\) for every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\); that
75	  is, the higher right-derived functors vanish on injective objects. In particular
76	  every injective object is right-\(G\)-acyclic in the sense of
77	  Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}.
78	\end{lemma}
79	
80	\begin{lemma}[The zeroth derived functor of a left-exact functor]
81	  \label{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
82	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedZeroIsoSelf}
83	  \mathlibok
84	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
85	  With \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, G\) as above, if \(G\) is left exact (preserves
86	  finite limits) then the natural transformation \(G \to R^0 G\) is an isomorphism,
87	  \[
88	    R^0 G \;\cong\; G.
89	  \]
90	\end{lemma}
91	
92	\begin{lemma}[Long exact homology sequence of a short exact sequence of complexes]
93	  \label{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
94	  \lean{CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.ShortExact.homology_exact₁,
95	    CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.ShortExact.homology_exact₂,
96	    CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.ShortExact.homology_exact₃,
97	    CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.ShortExact.δ}
98	  \mathlibok
99	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
100	  Let \(\mathcal{C}\) be an abelian category and let
101	  \[
102	    0 \longrightarrow S.X_1 \longrightarrow S.X_2 \longrightarrow S.X_3
103	      \longrightarrow 0
104	  \]
105	  be a short exact sequence of cochain complexes in \(\mathcal{C}\) --- that is, a
106	  short complex \(S = (S.X_1 \to S.X_2 \to S.X_3)\) of objects of the category of
107	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-indexed cochain complexes \(\mathrm{HomologicalComplex}\,\mathcal{C}\)
108	  which is short exact (degreewise mono, epi and exact in the middle). Then for every
109	  degree \(n\) there is a connecting morphism
110	  \[
111	    \delta^n \;:\; H^n(S.X_3) \longrightarrow H^{n+1}(S.X_1)
112	  \]
113	  (the Mathlib map \texttt{ShortComplex.ShortExact.\(\delta\)}), and these assemble
114	  into a long exact sequence of homology objects in \(\mathcal{C}\)
115	  \[
116	    \cdots \longrightarrow H^n(S.X_2) \longrightarrow H^n(S.X_3)
117	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^n\ } H^{n+1}(S.X_1)
118	      \longrightarrow H^{n+1}(S.X_2) \longrightarrow \cdots .
119	  \]
120	  This is the complex-level homology long exact sequence and its connecting
121	  homomorphism. The derived-functor long exact sequence used in the sequel is
122	  constructed from this complex-level result via a horseshoe lift; see
123	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}.
124	\end{lemma}
125	
126	\section{Right-acyclic objects}
127	
128	\begin{definition}\leanok
129	[Right-\(G\)-acyclic object]
130	  \label{def:right_acyclic}
131	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.IsRightAcyclic}
132	  \uses{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
133	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Definition
134	  % \texttt{definition-derived-functor} (Tag 0157, items 3--4) and Lemma
135	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic} (Tag 015C, part 2)
136	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5264--5269 and
137	  % L5594--5617).
138	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\item An object $A$ of $\mathcal{A}$ is said to be
139	  % {\it right acyclic for $F$}, or {\it acyclic for $RF$}
140	  % if $A[0]$ computes $RF$.
141	  % \item An object $A$ of $\mathcal{A}$ is said to be
142	  % {\it left acyclic for $F$}, or {\it acyclic for $LF$}
143	  % if $A[0]$ computes $LF$."
144	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be an additive functor
145	  % between abelian categories and assume
146	  % $RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere
147	  % defined. Let $A$ be an object of $\mathcal{A}$.
148	  % \begin{enumerate}
149	  % \item $A$ is right acyclic for $F$ if and only if
150	  % $F(A) \to R^0F(A)$ is an isomorphism and $R^iF(A) = 0$ for all $i > 0$,
151	  % \item if $F$ is left exact, then $A$ is right acyclic for $F$
152	  % if and only if $R^iF(A) = 0$ for all $i > 0$.
153	  % \end{enumerate}"
154	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 0157
155	  (\texttt{definition-derived-functor}) and Tag 015C
156	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic}).}
157	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be an additive functor between abelian
158	  categories. An object \(J\) of \(\mathcal{A}\) is \emph{right-\(G\)-acyclic} when
159	  the higher right-derived functors of \(G\) vanish at \(J\):
160	  \[
161	    (R^k G)(J) = 0 \qquad \text{for all } k \geq 1.
162	  \]
163	  Equivalently, writing the condition with the index shifted, \((R^{k+1} G)(J) = 0\)
164	  for all \(k \in \mathbb{N}\). The Stacks Project defines a right-\(G\)-acyclic
165	  object intrinsically (as one for which the one-term complex \(J[0]\) computes the
166	  derived functor \(RG\)); for an additive --- in our applications, left exact ---
167	  functor \(G\) that intrinsic condition is equivalent to the vanishing of all
168	  \((R^k G)(J)\) with \(k \geq 1\), which is the form we adopt here and which is
169	  directly checkable against the injective-resolution computation of
170	  Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}.
171	
172	  \emph{Remark (alternative quantifier shapes).} Two equivalent formulations are
173	  available: ``\((R^k G)(J) = 0\) for all \(k \geq 1\)'' or the index-shifted
174	  ``\((R^{k+1} G)(J) = 0\) for all \(k \in \mathbb{N}\)''; the latter avoids the
175	  inequality side-condition and aligns with the vanishing statement of
176	  Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}.
177	\end{definition}
178	
179	\section{Lifting a short exact sequence to injective resolutions}
180	
181	The passage from the complex-level homology long exact sequence
182	(Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) to a long exact sequence of
183	right-derived functors requires one geometric input: a short exact sequence of
184	objects must be lifted to a short exact sequence of their injective resolutions
185	which is, moreover, \emph{degreewise split}. This is the dual Horseshoe Lemma. It is the single genuinely new homological-algebra construction the argument
186	requires; individual injective resolutions (provided by enough injectives) furnish
187	the building blocks, and the horseshoe construction assembles them into the required
188	lift.

191	[Horseshoe lift of a short exact sequence to injective resolutions]
192	  \label{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
193	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact}

195	  % FALSE-DONE markers. CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact does NOT exist as a

198	  % "Output type (suggested): def InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact ...") which the LSP/sync
199	  % matcher reads as a real, sorry-free declaration. The horseshoe is the SOLE remaining P4

204	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives and let
205	  \[
206	    0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow B \longrightarrow C \longrightarrow 0
207	  \]
208	  be a short exact sequence in \(\mathcal{A}\). Then there exist injective
209	  resolutions \(I_A\) of \(A\), \(I_B\) of \(B\), \(I_C\) of \(C\), together with a
210	  short exact sequence of cochain complexes
211	  \[
212	    0 \longrightarrow I_A \longrightarrow I_B \longrightarrow I_C \longrightarrow 0
213	  \]
214	  which is compatible with the augmentations \(A \to I_A^0\), \(B \to I_B^0\),
215	  \(C \to I_C^0\) and which is \emph{degreewise split}: for every \(n\) the sequence
216	  \[
217	    0 \longrightarrow I_A^n \longrightarrow I_B^n \longrightarrow I_C^n
218	      \longrightarrow 0

221	  \(I_B^n \cong I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\).

225	  \uses{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
226	  \leanok
227	  This is the (dual) Horseshoe Lemma; see e.g.\ Weibel, \emph{An Introduction to
228	  Homological Algebra}, Lemma 2.2.8 (stated there for projective resolutions; the
229	  injective-resolution form is the dual). Choose injective resolutions
230	  \(A \to I_A^\bullet\) and \(C \to I_C^\bullet\), which exist because \(\mathcal{A}\)
231	  has enough injectives. Set \(I_B^n := I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\) in each degree; this is

# Refactor Directive

## Slug
fence-fix

## Problem
`AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/AcyclicResolution.lean` contains a `/-! ... -/` strategy
documentation comment whose body includes a backtick-fenced "suggested output type" code
block (around lines 281–287):

```
Output type (suggested):
```
def InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact {A B C : 𝒜} (ses : ShortComplex 𝒜)
    (hses : ses.ShortExact) (I_A : InjectiveResolution ses.X₁)
    (I_C : InjectiveResolution ses.X₃) :
    InjectiveResolution ses.X₂ × ShortComplex (CochainComplex 𝒜 ℕ)
```
or equivalently expose the SES directly as a field.
```

The `sync_leanok` declaration matcher (and `lean_file_outline` / `lean_local_search`) read
that fenced `def InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact ...` as a REAL, sorry-free declaration,
even though it is inert comment text and `lean_verify` reports the constant does not exist.
This produced a FALSE `\leanok` on the blueprint lemma `lem:injective_resolution_of_ses`
(whose `\lean{}` names `CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact`) — DAG poisoning:
the single hardest, still-unbuilt piece of phase P4 is being treated as done.

## Mathematical Justification
None needed — this is a pure documentation/formatting fix inside a comment. The declaration
`InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact` genuinely does NOT exist yet (it is the horseshoe object,
still to be built). The suggested-signature text is useful guidance for the future prover and
should be PRESERVED in meaning; it must merely be reformatted so that no line inside the
comment parses as a Lean declaration of that name.

## Changes Requested
- File: `AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/AcyclicResolution.lean`
  - Old: the backtick code-fence (` ``` `) wrapping the `def InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact
    ... : InjectiveResolution ses.X₂ × ShortComplex (CochainComplex 𝒜 ℕ)` signature inside the
    `/-! ... -/` strategy comment (around L281–287).
  - New: remove the ` ``` ` fences and reformat the signature as plain prose / `-- `-prefixed
    text so that NO line begins with `def InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact` (or any
    `def`/`theorem`/`lemma`/`abbrev`/`instance` + that name). Preserve the informational
    content (the suggested argument list and result type) — e.g. rewrite the opening as
    `-- Suggested output type (NOT yet defined): a function ofShortExact taking ses : ShortComplex 𝒜,`
    `-- hses : ses.ShortExact, I_A : InjectiveResolution ses.X₁, I_C : InjectiveResolution ses.X₃,`
    `-- and returning InjectiveResolution ses.X₂ together with a ShortComplex (CochainComplex 𝒜 ℕ).`
    The exact phrasing is yours; the only hard requirement is that the matcher no longer sees a
    declaration. Scan the WHOLE `/-! ... -/` strategy comment (lines ~197–347) for any OTHER
    backtick-fenced lines beginning with a declaration keyword and reformat those the same way.

## Affected Files
None downstream — the change is confined to a comment. No signatures, no proofs change. The
file must still compile (it already does; verify with `lean_diagnostic_messages`).

## Expected Outcome
- `AcyclicResolution.lean` compiles unchanged in behaviour; sorry count still 0.
- No line inside the `/-! ... -/` strategy comment parses as a `def`/`theorem`/etc. named
  `InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact` (or any other phantom declaration).
- After this lands, the next `sync_leanok` run will strip the false `\leanok` markers from
  `lem:injective_resolution_of_ses` automatically — do NOT edit the blueprint or any `\leanok`.

179	\section{Lifting a short exact sequence to injective resolutions}
180	
181	The passage from the complex-level homology long exact sequence
182	(Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) to a long exact sequence of
183	right-derived functors requires one geometric input: a short exact sequence of
184	objects must be lifted to a short exact sequence of their injective resolutions
185	which is, moreover, \emph{degreewise split}. This is the dual Horseshoe Lemma. It is the single genuinely new homological-algebra construction the argument
186	requires; individual injective resolutions (provided by enough injectives) furnish
187	the building blocks, and the horseshoe construction assembles them into the required
188	lift.
189	
190	\subsection{The horseshoe construction, step by step}
191	
192	The construction is a single \(\mathbb{N}\)-recursion (modelled on Mathlib's
193	\texttt{InjectiveResolution.ofCocomplex}) with no sorry-free partial fragment when
194	attempted wholesale. We therefore isolate its independent moving parts as the
195	sub-lemmas below; the target horseshoe lemma is then assembled from them. Throughout,
196	\(I_A : \mathrm{InjectiveResolution}\,A\) and \(I_C : \mathrm{InjectiveResolution}\,C\)
197	are fixed (they exist because \(\mathcal{A}\) has enough injectives), and we write
198	\(d_A, d_C\) for their differentials and \(I_A^n, I_C^n\) for their degree-\(n\) terms.
199	The middle resolution is built degreewise as the biproduct
200	\(I_B^n := I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\). All seven statements are structural pieces of the dual
201	Horseshoe Lemma (dual of Weibel, \emph{An Introduction to Homological Algebra}, Lemma
202	2.2.8); no new source is introduced.
203	
204	\begin{lemma}[Degree term: a finite biproduct of injectives is injective]
205	  \label{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}
206	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Injective.instBiprod}
207	  \mathlibok
208	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
209	  In an abelian category, if \(P\) and \(Q\) are injective objects then their biproduct
210	  \(P \oplus Q\) is injective. Consequently each degree term
211	  \(I_B^n := I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\) of the middle complex is injective, being the
212	  biproduct of the injective terms \(I_A^n\) and \(I_C^n\) of the two chosen
213	  resolutions.
214	\end{lemma}
215	
216	\begin{lemma}[Degree term: the biproduct short complex is split exact]
217	  \label{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}
218	  \lean{CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.Splitting.ofHasBinaryBiproduct}
219	  \mathlibok
220	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
221	  For objects \(P, Q\) of an abelian category the short complex
222	  \[
223	    P \xrightarrow{\ \iota_P\ } P \oplus Q \xrightarrow{\ \pi_Q\ } Q
224	  \]
225	  formed from the biproduct coprojection \(\iota_P\) and projection \(\pi_Q\) carries a
226	  canonical \texttt{ShortComplex.Splitting}; in particular it is a split short exact
227	  sequence. Applied in each degree \(n\) with \(P = I_A^n\), \(Q = I_C^n\), this exhibits
228	  every degree
229	  \[
230	    0 \longrightarrow I_A^n \longrightarrow I_B^n \longrightarrow I_C^n \longrightarrow 0
231	  \]
232	  as split, supplying exactly the degreewise-splitting data the downstream comparison
233	  (\(\mathrm{rightDerivedShiftIsoOfSplitResolutionSES}\)) consumes.
234	\end{lemma}
235	
236	\begin{lemma}[Per-stage monomorphism into the biproduct]
237	  \label{lem:horseshoe_stage_mono}
238	  \lean{CategoryTheory.mono_biprod_lift_factorThru_of_exact}
239	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}
240	  Let \(0 \to S.X_1 \xrightarrow{S.f} S.X_2 \xrightarrow{S.g} S.X_3 \to 0\) be an exact
241	  short complex with \(S.f\) a monomorphism, and let \(\alpha : S.X_1 \to P\),
242	  \(\gamma : S.X_3 \to Q\) be monomorphisms with \(P\) injective. Then the map
243	  \[
244	    \bigl\langle\, \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f)\,,\; S.g \circ \gamma \,\bigr\rangle
245	      \;:\; S.X_2 \longrightarrow P \oplus Q,
246	  \]
247	  whose first component is the injective extension of \(\alpha\) along the mono \(S.f\)
248	  and whose second component is \(S.X_2 \twoheadrightarrow S.X_3 \hookrightarrow Q\), is a
249	  monomorphism. This is the kernel/cokernel step driving each stage of the recursion:
250	  applied to the \(n\)-th cosyzygy short exact sequence with \(P = I_A^{n+1}\),
251	  \(Q = I_C^{n+1}\) it produces the monomorphism of the next cosyzygy into the next
252	  biproduct term, whose cokernel feeds the following stage (and, at the base stage with
253	  the original sequence \(0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0\), the augmentation
254	  \(\beta : B \hookrightarrow I_B^0\)).
255	\end{lemma}
256	
257	\begin{proof}
258	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}
259	  Write \(u := \langle \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f), S.g \circ \gamma\rangle\). To see
260	  \(u\) is mono it suffices, in an abelian category, to show \(x \circ u = 0 \implies x = 0\)
261	  for any \(x\). Composing \(x \circ u\) with the two biproduct projections gives
262	  \(x \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f) = 0\) and \(x \circ S.g \circ \gamma = 0\);
263	  since \(\gamma\) is mono the latter yields \(x \circ S.g = 0\). Exactness of \(S\) at
264	  \(S.X_2\) (with \(S.f\) mono) lets us lift \(x\) through \(S.f\): there is \(y\) with
265	  \(y \circ S.f = x\). Then \(y \circ \alpha = y \circ S.f \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f)
266	  = x \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f) = 0\), using the defining property
267	  \(S.f \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f) = \alpha\); as \(\alpha\) is mono, \(y = 0\),
268	  hence \(x = y \circ S.f = 0\).
269	\end{proof}
270	
271	\begin{lemma}[Off-diagonal twist and augmentation lifts]
272	  \label{lem:horseshoe_twist}
273	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact_twist}
274	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_stage_mono}
275	  There exist an augmentation \(\beta : B \to I_B^0\) and a family of morphisms
276	  \[
277	    \tau^n : I_C^n \longrightarrow I_A^{n+1} \qquad (n \in \mathbb{N})
278	  \]
279	  --- the off-diagonal components of the middle differential --- satisfying the cocycle
280	  identity
281	  \[
282	    \tau^{n+1} \circ d_C^n \;=\; -\, d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n \qquad (n \in \mathbb{N}),
283	  \]
284	  together with the compatibility of \(\beta\) with the augmentations \(A \to I_A^0\),
285	  \(C \to I_C^0\) and the maps of the original short exact sequence. Each \(\tau^n\) (and
286	  \(\beta\)) is produced by the universal lifting property of injectives
287	  (\(\mathrm{Injective.factorThru}\)): the target summand \(I_A^{n+1}\) is injective and
288	  the relevant source map is a monomorphism (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_stage_mono}).
289	\end{lemma}
290	
291	\begin{proof}
292	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_stage_mono, lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
293	  The lifts are constructed by recursion on \(n\). For the augmentation, the first
294	  component of \(\beta\) extends the composite \(A \to I_A^0\) along the monomorphism
295	  \(A \hookrightarrow B\) using injectivity of \(I_A^0\) (this is
296	  \(\mathrm{factorThru}\)); the second component is \(B \twoheadrightarrow C \to I_C^0\).
297	  At the inductive step we have \(\tau^{n}\) (with \(\tau^{n} : I_C^{n} \to I_A^{n+1}\))
298	  and seek \(\tau^{n+1} : I_C^{n+1} \to I_A^{n+2}\) with
299	  \(\tau^{n+1} \circ d_C^n = -\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n\). The right-hand side
300	  \(-\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n : I_C^n \to I_A^{n+2}\) annihilates
301	  \(\ker d_C^n = \operatorname{im} d_C^{n-1}\): precomposing with \(d_C^{n-1}\) and using the
302	  previous-stage identity \(\tau^{n} \circ d_C^{n-1} = -\,d_A^{n} \circ \tau^{n-1}\) gives
303	  \(-\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n \circ d_C^{n-1} = d_A^{n+1} \circ d_A^{n} \circ \tau^{n-1} = 0\)
304	  (exactness of \(I_C\) at degree \(n\),
305	  Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}). Hence
306	  \(-\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n\) factors through the image of \(d_C^n\), i.e.\ through the
307	  monomorphism \(\operatorname{im} d_C^n \hookrightarrow I_C^{n+1}\); injectivity of the
308	  target \(I_A^{n+2}\) extends that factorization along this monomorphism to a map
309	  \(\tau^{n+1} : I_C^{n+1} \to I_A^{n+2}\) defined on all of \(I_C^{n+1}\) and satisfying
310	  \(\tau^{n+1} \circ d_C^n = -\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n\) by construction. This is exactly
311	  the dual of the inductive lift in the Horseshoe Lemma.
312	\end{proof}
313	
314	\begin{lemma}[The assembled middle differential squares to zero]
315	  \label{lem:horseshoe_dComp}
316	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact_dComp}
317	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_twist}
318	  With the twist family \(\tau\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_twist}, define the middle
319	  differential degreewise by the matrix
320	  \[
321	    d_B^n \;=\;
322	    \begin{pmatrix} d_A^n & \tau^n \\ 0 & d_C^n \end{pmatrix}
323	    \;:\; I_A^n \oplus I_C^n \longrightarrow I_A^{n+1} \oplus I_C^{n+1}.
324	  \]
325	  Then \(d_B^{n+1} \circ d_B^n = 0\) for every \(n\), so \((I_B^\bullet, d_B)\) is a
326	  cochain complex.
327	\end{lemma}
328	
329	\begin{proof}
330	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_twist}
331	  Multiplying the two matrices, the composite \(d_B^{n+1} \circ d_B^n\) has diagonal
332	  entries \(d_A^{n+1} \circ d_A^n = 0\) and \(d_C^{n+1} \circ d_C^n = 0\) (the complexes
333	  \(I_A\), \(I_C\) being complexes), lower-left entry \(0\), and upper-right entry
334	  \[
335	    d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n + \tau^{n+1} \circ d_C^n,
336	  \]
337	  which vanishes precisely by the cocycle identity
338	  \(\tau^{n+1} \circ d_C^n = -\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n\) of
339	  Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_twist}. Hence \(d_B^{n+1} \circ d_B^n = 0\).
340	\end{proof}
341	
342	\begin{lemma}[The inclusion and projection are chain maps]
343	  \label{lem:horseshoe_chainMap}
344	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact_chainMap}
345	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_dComp}
346	  With \(d_B\) as in Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_dComp}, the degreewise biproduct
347	  coprojection \(\iota^n = \iota_{I_A^n} : I_A^n \to I_B^n\) and projection
348	  \(\pi^n = \pi_{I_C^n} : I_B^n \to I_C^n\) assemble into chain maps
349	  \(\iota : I_A^\bullet \to I_B^\bullet\) and \(\pi : I_B^\bullet \to I_C^\bullet\); that
350	  is, \(d_B^n \circ \iota^n = \iota^{n+1} \circ d_A^n\) and
351	  \(\pi^{n+1} \circ d_B^n = d_C^n \circ \pi^n\). Moreover \(\iota, \pi\) are compatible
352	  with the augmentations and form, in each degree, the split short exact sequence
353	  \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_B^n \to I_C^n \to 0\) of
354	  Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}.
355	\end{lemma}
356	
357	\begin{proof}
The decomposition is excellent and detailed. Let me read the rest (resolvesMiddle + dimension-shift section) to place the coverage hints.

358	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_dComp, lem:horseshoe_degree_split}
359	  These are direct biproduct computations from the matrix form of \(d_B\). Pre-composing
360	  \(d_B^n\) with the coprojection \(\iota^n\) of the first summand selects the first
361	  column \(\begin{pmatrix} d_A^n \\ 0\end{pmatrix}\), which equals
362	  \(\iota^{n+1} \circ d_A^n\); so \(\iota\) is a chain map. Post-composing \(d_B^n\) with
363	  the projection \(\pi^{n+1}\) onto the second summand selects the second row
364	  \(\begin{pmatrix} 0 & d_C^n\end{pmatrix}\), which equals \(d_C^n \circ \pi^n\); so
365	  \(\pi\) is a chain map. Compatibility with the augmentations is the corresponding
366	  statement in degree \(-1 \to 0\), immediate from the construction of \(\beta\) in
367	  Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_twist}. In each degree \(\iota^n = \iota_{I_A^n}\) and
368	  \(\pi^n = \pi_{I_C^n}\) are the structure maps of the biproduct, so the degree is split
369	  exact by Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}.
370	\end{proof}

372	\begin{lemma}[The middle complex resolves \(B\)]
373	  \label{lem:horseshoe_resolvesMiddle}
374	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact_resolvesMiddle}
375	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_chainMap, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
376	  The cochain complex \((I_B^\bullet, d_B)\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_dComp}, with the
377	  augmentation \(\beta : B \to I_B^0\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_twist}, is an injective
378	  resolution of \(B\): its terms are injective
379	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}), it is exact in every positive degree, and
380	  \(H^0 \cong B\) via \(\beta\).
381	\end{lemma}
382	
383	\begin{proof}
384	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_chainMap, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
385	    lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}
386	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_chainMap} the maps \(\iota, \pi\) form, degreewise, the
387	  split short exact sequence \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_B^n \to I_C^n \to 0\); assembling these
388	  splittings (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}) gives a short exact sequence of
389	  cochain complexes
390	  \[
391	    0 \longrightarrow I_A^\bullet \longrightarrow I_B^\bullet
392	      \longrightarrow I_C^\bullet \longrightarrow 0 .
393	  \]
394	  Apply the complex-level homology long exact sequence
395	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}). The outer complexes \(I_A^\bullet\),
396	  \(I_C^\bullet\) are injective resolutions, hence exact in every positive degree
397	  (\(H^{k}(I_A) = H^{k}(I_C) = 0\) for \(k \geq 1\)) with \(H^0(I_A) \cong A\),
398	  \(H^0(I_C) \cong C\). In the long exact sequence the terms flanking \(H^{k}(I_B)\) for
399	  \(k \geq 1\) vanish, forcing \(H^{k}(I_B) = 0\); in degree \(0\) the initial segment
400	  \(0 \to H^0(I_A) \to H^0(I_B) \to H^0(I_C) \to H^1(I_A) = 0\) reads
401	  \(0 \to A \to H^0(I_B) \to C \to 0\), which together with the original sequence
402	  \(0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0\) and the augmentation \(\beta\) identifies
403	  \(H^0(I_B) \cong B\). Hence \(I_B^\bullet\) is exact in positive degrees and resolves
404	  \(B\); with injective terms it is an injective resolution of \(B\).
405	\end{proof}

464	\section{The dimension-shift step}
465	
466	The induction driving the comparison theorem rests on a single short-exact-sequence
467	calculation: cutting a resolution into short exact sequences and reading off the
468	long exact sequence of right-derived functors. The latter is not available off the
469	shelf at the level of the derived-functor objects \((R^k G)\); we build it from the
470	complex-level homology long exact sequence
471	(Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) via the horseshoe lift
472	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}). We isolate the result as the kernel
473	sub-lemma below.

497	  % is a short exact sequence of $\mathcal{B}$."
498	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015D
499	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}).}
500	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be an additive left-exact functor, and let
501	  \[
502	    0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow J \longrightarrow Z \longrightarrow 0
503	  \]
504	  be a short exact sequence in \(\mathcal{A}\) with the middle term \(J\)
505	  right-\(G\)-acyclic. Then:
506	  \begin{enumerate}
507	    \item the connecting maps of the long exact sequence of right-derived functors
508	      furnish isomorphisms
509	      \[
510	        (R^{k} G)(Z) \;\xrightarrow{\ \cong\ }\; (R^{k+1} G)(A)
511	        \qquad \text{for all } k \geq 1;
512	      \]
513	    \item in the lowest degree there is an isomorphism
514	      \[
515	        (R^{1} G)(A) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J) \to G(Z)\bigr),
516	      \]
517	      i.e.\ \((R^1 G)(A)\) is the cokernel of the map \(G(J) \to G(Z)\) induced by
518	      the surjection \(J \twoheadrightarrow Z\).
519	  \end{enumerate}
520	\end{lemma}
521	
522	% The Stacks proof of Tag 015D derives the long exact sequence from the
523	% delta-functor structure of $RF$ on the derived category $D^{+}(\mathcal{A})$.
524	% The proof below constructs the same sequence at the level of right-derived-functor
525	% objects from the complex-level homology long exact sequence
526	% (Lemma \ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) after a horseshoe lift
527	% (Lemma \ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}); accordingly no verbatim proof
528	% quote from Tag 015D is attached.
529	\begin{proof}
530	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
531	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
532	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
533	  We first construct the long exact sequence of right-derived functors associated
534	  to \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\). In the classical treatment this sequence is
535	  supplied by the \(\delta\)-functor structure of \(\{R^k G\}\) on the derived
536	  category; here it is built directly from the complex-level homology long exact
537	  sequence via a horseshoe lift.
538	
539	  \emph{Building the long exact sequence.} By the horseshoe lift
540	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}) choose injective resolutions
541	  \(I_A, I_J, I_Z\) of \(A, J, Z\) fitting into a short exact sequence of cochain
542	  complexes
543	  \[
544	    0 \longrightarrow I_A \longrightarrow I_J \longrightarrow I_Z \longrightarrow 0
545	  \]
546	  that is degreewise split. Apply \(G\) degreewise. Because each degree
547	  \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_J^n \to I_Z^n \to 0\) is a \emph{split} short exact sequence
548	  (so \(I_J^n \cong I_A^n \oplus I_Z^n\) compatibly with the maps), the additive
549	  functor \(G\) carries it to a split short exact sequence
550	  \(0 \to G(I_A^n) \to G(I_J^n) \to G(I_Z^n) \to 0\) --- this is where degreewise
551	  splitness is essential, since \(G\) is not assumed exact and would not in general
552	  preserve a non-split short exact sequence. Hence
553	  \[
554	    0 \longrightarrow G(I_A) \longrightarrow G(I_J) \longrightarrow G(I_Z)
555	      \longrightarrow 0
556	  \]
557	  is a short exact sequence of cochain complexes in \(\mathcal{B}\). The
558	  complex-level homology long exact sequence
559	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) applied to it reads
560	  \[
561	    \cdots \to H^k\bigl(G(I_J)\bigr) \to H^k\bigl(G(I_Z)\bigr)
562	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } H^{k+1}\bigl(G(I_A)\bigr)
563	      \to H^{k+1}\bigl(G(I_J)\bigr) \to \cdots .
564	  \]
565	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution} the homology of each applied
566	  resolution computes the right-derived functor:
567	  \(H^k(G(I_A)) \cong (R^k G)(A)\), \(H^k(G(I_J)) \cong (R^k G)(J)\) and
568	  \(H^k(G(I_Z)) \cong (R^k G)(Z)\), naturally in the maps of the sequence. Transporting
569	  the homology long exact sequence across these isomorphisms gives the long exact
570	  sequence of right-derived functors
571	  \[
572	    \cdots \to (R^k G)(J) \to (R^k G)(Z)
573	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } (R^{k+1} G)(A) \to (R^{k+1} G)(J) \to \cdots,
574	  \]
575	  and, using \(R^0 G \cong G\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) at the
576	  bottom, its low-degree initial segment
577	  \[
578	    0 \to G(A) \to G(J) \to G(Z)
579	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to (R^1 G)(J) \to \cdots .
580	  \]
581	
582	  \emph{Killing the acyclic terms.} Because \(J\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic
583	  (Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}), every term \((R^k G)(J)\) with \(k \geq 1\)
584	  vanishes. For \(k \geq 1\) the segment around \((R^k G)(J)\),
585	  \[
586	    \underbrace{(R^{k} G)(J)}_{=0} \to (R^{k} G)(Z)
587	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } (R^{k+1} G)(A) \to \underbrace{(R^{k+1} G)(J)}_{=0},
588	  \]
589	  has both outer terms zero, so the connecting map
590	  \(\delta^k : (R^{k} G)(Z) \to (R^{k+1} G)(A)\) is an isomorphism; this is part (1).
591	  In the lowest degree the segment
592	  \[
593	    G(J) \to G(Z) \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A)
594	      \to \underbrace{(R^1 G)(J)}_{=0}
595	  \]
596	  is exact with vanishing right end, so \(\delta^0\) is an epimorphism whose kernel is
597	  the image of \(G(J) \to G(Z)\); hence \(\delta^0\) identifies
598	  \(\operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J) \to G(Z)\bigr)\) with \((R^1 G)(A)\), which is
599	  part (2).
600	\end{proof}
601	
602	\section{The acyclic-resolution comparison theorem}
603	
604	\begin{theorem}[An acyclic resolution computes the right-derived functor]
605	  \label{lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived}

## Slug
horseshoe

## Chapter
`blueprint/src/chapters/Cohomology_AcyclicResolution.tex`

## Context
An `effort-breaker` round this iteration decomposed the monolithic dual-Horseshoe lemma
(`lem:injective_resolution_of_ses`) into a new `\subsection{The horseshoe construction,
step by step}` (chapter L190–462) containing seven `\uses`-linked sub-lemmas
(`lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective`, `…_degree_split`, `…_stage_mono`, `…_twist`, `…_dComp`,
`…_chainMap`, `…_resolvesMiddle`). Clean the chapter — concentrate on that new subsection,
but pass over the whole file.

## Tasks (your standard gate)
1. **Strip Lean/process leakage.** The new subsection and its intro contain
   implementation-process phrasing that must become timeless mathematics — e.g.:
   - "is a single \(\mathbb{N}\)-recursion (modelled on Mathlib's
     \texttt{InjectiveResolution.ofCocomplex}) with no sorry-free partial fragment when
     attempted wholesale. We therefore isolate its independent moving parts …" (L192–195) —
     rewrite as a mathematical statement (the construction proceeds by induction on degree;
     we isolate its parts as the lemmas below), dropping the Lean/prover-process framing
     ("sorry-free partial fragment", "attempted wholesale", "modelled on Mathlib's …").
   - Any other "the prover", "the recursion bookkeeping", `\texttt{InjectiveResolution.…}`
     Mathlib-name mentions used as *implementation* notes (as opposed to a genuine
     `\mathlibok` dependency anchor) → convert to plain mathematics or remove.
   - The `% NOTE (iter-004 review): … FALSE-DONE markers …` comment block at L411–419 is a
     review annotation about a marker-sync artifact; LEAVE IT (it is a `%`-comment, harmless
     in output, and documents a known issue being resolved this iter). Do NOT remove it.
2. **Citation discipline.** The two `\mathlibok` anchors (`lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective`
   → `Injective.instBiprod`; `lem:horseshoe_degree_split` →
   `ShortComplex.Splitting.ofHasBinaryBiproduct`) are Mathlib dependency anchors and need no
   `% SOURCE QUOTE` (they are Mathlib-provided, not external-source results). The new
   project sub-lemmas are pieces of the dual Horseshoe Lemma (dual of Weibel, *An
   Introduction to Homological Algebra*, Lemma 2.2.8); the parent block already carries that
   inline citation and the sub-lemmas inherit it — do NOT fabricate a verbatim `% SOURCE
   QUOTE` for them (none exists; the horseshoe is the one genuinely-new structural
   construction). Only add/repair a quote where a block genuinely derives from a source file
   already in `references/`.
3. **Trim verbosity** in the new subsection where prose is redundant, keeping every
   mathematical step.
4. **Fix LaTeX** — confirm balanced `\begin`/`\end`, braces, and that all `\uses{}` /
   `\label{}` cross-references resolve.

## Do NOT
- Do NOT touch `\leanok` markers anywhere (sync_leanok owns them; the false markers on
  `lem:injective_resolution_of_ses` are being auto-resolved this iter via a Lean-side fence
  fix — leave them).
- Do NOT change any `\lean{}` target name or any `\label{}`.
- Do NOT weaken or restate any lemma; this is a purity/format pass only.

96	  ### Strategy / multi-route handling
97

109	
110	  Deferring a prover round is the correct, intentional action — not a
111	  failure. The 1-iter latency cost of waiting for a writer is small
112	  compared to the cost of a prover formalizing a broken blueprint and

116	
117	# Blueprint Reviewer
118	
119	You read the **entire blueprint** plus a context bundle from the plan agent and produce a per-chapter checklist + a set of cross-cutting summaries. You are **read-only on project source** and the blueprint — your only writable target is your own report under `task_results/`.
120	
121	You are **mandatory in the plan phase**: the plan agent dispatches you every iteration before writing prover objectives. Your output is the plan agent's primary window into blueprint health, so the plan agent doesn't have to read every chapter itself.
122	
123	## Your Job
124	
125	The plan agent gives you a directive containing the current strategy snapshot, the references you should treat as authoritative, and any specific concerns. You then audit:
126	
127	- **Completeness** — for each chapter, are the definitions / theorems the strategy says the project needs actually present? Is the proof sketch detailed enough for a prover to formalize without guessing? Are the cross-references (`\uses{...}`) accurate?
128	- **Correctness** — does any definition contradict its references? Does any proof sketch contain a step that doesn't follow? Does any `\lean{...}` hint name a declaration that doesn't exist or has the wrong signature?
129	- **Dependency-graph integrity (use `leandag`)** — the `\uses{}` edges must reflect the real mathematical dependencies. `leandag` computes the actual DAG, so use it instead of eyeballing:
130	  - **Broken edges** — `leandag build --json` reports `unknown_uses` (a `\uses{}` pointing at a label no declaration defines). Every one is a must-fix.
131	  - **Missing edges** — read each proof: if it relies on a lemma/definition that its `\uses{}` does not list, the edge is missing. These don't show up as "broken"; you find them by reading the math against the declared `\uses{}`. Missing edges make the loop dispatch provers out of order, so flag them as correctness findings.
132	  - **Isolated declarations** — `leandag show isolated` (or `leandag query --isolated --type theorem`, and the `isolated` / `isolated_blueprint` counts in `leandag stats`) lists nodes with **no `\uses{}` out and nothing using them**. An isolated *blueprint* node is almost always a *symptom of a missing edge*, not dead weight — so for each one decide and record a disposition (see below). Isolated `lean_aux` nodes are uncovered Lean helpers, a separate "needs a blueprint entry" signal, not removal candidates.
133	- **Mathlib dependency anchors (`\mathlibok`)** — it is good practice for a chapter that relies on a Mathlib-provided result to state it as an explicit anchor block marked `\mathlibok` (with `\lean{}` naming the real Mathlib declaration), rather than a bare `\uses{}` to nothing. Treat such anchors as *valid and done* — never flag them as ∞ holes or `remove` candidates. But **audit each `\mathlibok` claim for faithfulness**, because a wrong one makes the loop skip a real gap: does Mathlib actually contain the named `\lean{}` declaration, and does the stated form match it? Verify against Lean (`archon-lean-lsp`) when in doubt. A `\mathlibok` on a statement Mathlib does not provide — or whose form is stronger/different than Mathlib's — is a hard fail (treat like a fabricated citation). Also: where a broken `\uses{}` points at what is really a Mathlib result, the right `wire-up` is "add a `\mathlibok` anchor for it," not "invent a project lemma."
134	- **Lean target formulation quality** — for each `\lean{...}` hint, is the named theorem/definition a *useful* target for the prover? Vague or under-specified hints lead to wrong formalizations; surface those.
135	- **Rendering integrity (run `archon blueprint-doctor`)** — the blueprint must *render*: as the compiled leanblueprint site and in the dashboard. The doctor lints this deterministically — run `archon blueprint-doctor --json` once and triage every `malformed_refs` finding into your report:
136	  - **`undefined-macro`** — a chapter uses `\foo` that no `macros/*.tex` or chapter-local `\providecommand` defines: it renders as raw TeX everywhere. Recommend the definition (usually obvious from context: `\Z` → `\mathbb{Z}`, `\fppf` → `\mathrm{fppf}`); when the intent is genuinely unclear (you cannot tell what the writer meant), flag it as a correctness finding for the chapter's writer instead of guessing.
137	  - **`math-delim`** — interleaved `$ … \( … \) … $` delimiters that shred formulas mid-sentence; per-line locations are in the finding. Must-fix, writer-directive material.
138	  - **`literal-ref` / `bare-label`** — placeholder "REF" tokens and raw label ids in prose; each needs a `\cref{}` or the human-readable number.
139	  Group these per chapter in your findings so the dispatcher can scope one writer per damaged chapter.
140	- **Multi-route coverage** — if the strategy lists multiple viable routes (alternative proof approaches, alternative definitions), is each route represented in the blueprint? Routes the strategy mentions but the blueprint does not cover are red flags.
141	- **Unstarted phases** — for each phase in the strategy that has no blueprint chapter at all, produce a concrete chapter outline (see "Unstarted-phase proposals" below). This is a proactive planning function, not just a gap report.
142	- **Citation discipline** — for every definition / theorem / lemma block that derives from external reference material, audit all four elements:
143	  1. **`% SOURCE:` pointer with local-file parenthetical.** Format must be `% SOURCE: <pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. Verify the named local file EXISTS under `references/`. A `% SOURCE:` with no `(read from …)` parenthetical, or with a parenthetical naming a file that doesn't exist on disk, is a hard fail — the writer fabricated the citation.
144	  2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` verbatim text** for definitions / theorems / lemmas. Audit dimensions:
145	     - **Original language**: the quote must be in the source's original language. A quote in English when the source is Bourbaki / EGA (French) signals translation, which is not allowed — flag it.
146	     - **Original notation**: the quote must use the source's notation, even when it differs from the project's. If the project writes $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$ everywhere but the `% SOURCE QUOTE:` also writes $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$ when the source is Hartshorne (who writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$), the quote was rewritten — flag it.
147	     - **Verbatim, every word**: a quote that reads like a paraphrase ("essentially says that …", "the source states …") rather than direct copy is a hard fail. The whole point of the verbatim is anti-hallucination; paraphrased quotes are exactly the failure mode.
148	  3. **`% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`** immediately before the `\begin{proof}` environment, when the block has a proof and the proof derives from the source. Same verbatim rules as `% SOURCE QUOTE:`. Missing `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` on a theorem whose proof clearly comes from the cited source is a citation-discipline finding. (Archon-original proofs of external statements are allowed — flag only when the proof prose itself reads as a translation of an obvious source proof.)
149	  4. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}`** line as the first line of the block's prose. Missing → flag.
150	
151	  Cross-check: the visible `\textit{Source: ...}` pointer must match the `% SOURCE:` pointer. Drift between them signals copy-paste error or hallucination.
152	
153	  Spot-check against `## References consulted` in the corresponding writer's report (when available in `task_results/`): every distinct `references/<file>.md` named in `% SOURCE:` parentheticals across the chapter should appear in that list. A `% SOURCE: ... (read from references/X.md)` where the writer's "References consulted" list does NOT mention `references/X.md` means the writer cited a file they did not actually open this session — fabrication.
154	
155	  **Archon-original / project-bespoke** results (no external source) omit the source lines entirely — do not falsely flag those. The signal that a block is Archon-original: the directive that produced it didn't name an external source, or the chapter prose explicitly characterizes it as new (e.g. "This is the technical heart of our argument"). When in doubt, ask the plan agent in "Notes for Plan Agent" rather than flagging.
156	
157	You audit the blueprint **against the context the plan agent gave you**, not against your own opinions about how the math should be set up. But you are critical of weak prose — under-specified blueprints fail provers and are not safe to merge.
158	
159	`/references/summary.md` lists the project's reference materials. The planner writes the blueprints, while its knowledge might be enough to write some parts of the blueprint, some parts may be subject to hallucination and require reference material. If you believe a reference is required to write mathematically correct and complete blueprint chapters, you should mention it in your report so that the planner can retrieve it for the writer.
160	
161	## Always read everything
162	
163	**Read every chapter under `blueprint/src/chapters/`**, no exceptions, regardless of project size. The cross-chapter view is the entire reason for running me. If the directive contains a "scope" hint, treat it as a focus suggestion (which chapters need extra attention), not as a permission to skip reading.
164	
165	## Unstarted-phase proposals
166	
167	This is a proactive function — you produce it in addition to the per-chapter audit, not instead of it.
168	
169	After completing the per-chapter checklist, cross-reference the strategy snapshot's `## Phases & estimations` table against the set of chapters you just read. (That table lists only *remaining* phases — finished ones live in `## Completed` and need no proposal.) For each remaining phase row whose content has **zero blueprint coverage** (no chapter exists, or the only existing chapter is a stub with fewer than ~3 meaningful declaration blocks), produce a **chapter outline proposal**.
170	
171	A chapter outline proposal is not a flag or a complaint — it is a concrete, actionable seed that the plan agent can hand directly to a blueprint-writing subagent. It must contain enough mathematical detail that a writer receiving it as a directive can produce a complete chapter without further research by the plan agent.
172	
173	The proposal answers:
174	
175	1. **Which Lean file(s) will this chapter cover?** Name the expected file path(s) and whether this should be a consolidated chapter (one chapter covering multiple files via `% archon:covers`) or a 1:1 chapter.
176	
177	2. **What are the declaration blocks the chapter needs?** For each: the block type (definition / lemma / theorem / proposition), a proposed `\label`, a one-sentence description of the mathematical content, the expected `\lean{...}` hint (even if speculative — tag it `[expected]`), and the likely reference source (from `references/summary.md` or a named standard reference). Do not write the full prose — that is the writer's job — but give enough that the writer knows exactly what to write.
178	
179	3. **What are the internal dependencies?** Which declarations within this chapter use others (the `\uses{...}` graph in skeletal form). Identifying this upfront prevents the writer from writing declarations in an order that creates circular `\uses` references.
180	
181	4. **What is the proof strategy for the chapter's main theorem(s)?** Two to four sentences naming the key steps, the supporting lemmas, and any Mathlib infrastructure the proofs will depend on. This is the piece most likely to require a reference; if you can identify the relevant section of a source in `references/summary.md`, name it.
182	
183	5. **What references should the blueprint-writer read first?** List the specific local files under `references/` that are most relevant, and the sections within them. If no local file covers the material, flag it as a retrieval need so the plan agent can dispatch a reference-retriever before the writer.
184
Now the whole-blueprint reviewer for the HARD GATE, with context on the known marker artifact.

295	  and seek \(\tau^{n+1} : I_C^{n+1} \to I_A^{n+2}\) with
296	  \(\tau^{n+1} \circ d_C^n = -\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n\). The right-hand side
297	  \(-\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n : I_C^n \to I_A^{n+2}\) annihilates
298	  \(\ker d_C^n = \operatorname{im} d_C^{n-1}\): precomposing with \(d_C^{n-1}\) and using the

307	  \(\tau^{n+1} \circ d_C^n = -\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n\) by construction. This is exactly
308	  the dual of the inductive lift in the Horseshoe Lemma.

  \(-\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n \circ d_C^{n-1} = d_A^{n+1} \circ d_A^{n} \circ \tau^{n-1} = 0\),
  where the final equality is the complex identity \(d_A^{n+1} \circ d_A^{n} = 0\) for the
  cochain complex \(I_A\). Hence

# Project Progress

## Current Stage

prover

## Stages
- [x] init
- [x] autoformalize
- [ ] prover
- [ ] polish

## End-state overview

**Zero inline `sorry` in the dependency cone of the protected declaration
`AlgebraicGeometry.cech_computes_higherDirectImage`, kernel-only axioms, 0 project
axioms.** Sole goal: close the Čech computation of higher direct images
(`lem:cech_computes_cohomology`) and its cone. Full framing in STRATEGY.md.

## Iter-005 outcome — horseshoe DECOMPOSED + DAG-poisoning fixed; P4 prover dispatched on the new leaves

iter-004 built every *consumer* of the horseshoe and collapsed P4 to a single remaining
construction — the horseshoe object `InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact` — which the prover
correctly declined as a monolithic ℕ-recursion. This iter:
- **effort-breaker** decomposed `lem:injective_resolution_of_ses` into a 7-link `\uses`-chain:
  2 verified `\mathlibok` anchors (`Injective.instBiprod`, `ShortComplex.Splitting.ofHasBinaryBiproduct`),
  1 existing-project-decl anchor (`mono_biprod_lift_factorThru_of_exact`), and **4 NEW provable
  sub-goals** (`…ofShortExact_twist` [the recursion kernel], `…_dComp`, `…_chainMap`,
  `…_resolvesMiddle`).
- **refactor** reformatted the backtick code-fence in `AcyclicResolution.lean` that was poisoning
  the DAG (false `\leanok` on the horseshoe via `sync_leanok` matcher); sync_leanok will strip the
  false markers automatically this iter.
- **blueprint-clean** + **whole blueprint-reviewer** → `Cohomology_AcyclicResolution.tex` PASSES
  the HARD GATE (`complete: true`, `correct: true`; both `\mathlibok` anchors verified faithful;
  all 7 sub-lemma sketches adequate; `lem:horseshoe_twist` needs NO finer re-break). The one "soon"
  finding (imprecise exactness citation in the twist proof) was fixed directly in the blueprint.
- **progress-critic**: UNCLEAR (route fresh, 1 effective prover iter) — proceed; treat the
  same-iter re-review + prover dispatch as the correct response to the monolith, not avoidance.

## Current Objectives

1. **`AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/AcyclicResolution.lean`** — **Scaffold and build the four NEW
   horseshoe sub-goal declarations (they do not yet exist as Lean declarations), then assemble the
   horseshoe and finish the P4 chain — bottom-up, axiom-clean, no `sorry`.** The file compiles
   (0 sorries); all *consumers* of the horseshoe are already proven and wired
   (`rightDerivedShiftIsoOfSplitResolutionSES` etc.). Blueprint:
   `chapters/Cohomology_AcyclicResolution.tex`, §"The horseshoe construction, step by step"
   (every sub-lemma has a detailed, gate-passed proof sketch). Build in dependency order:
   1. **`CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact_twist`** (`lem:horseshoe_twist` — the
      recursion kernel): the off-diagonal twist family `τⁿ : I_C^n ⟶ I_A^{n+1}` and augmentation
      `β : B ⟶ I_B^0` with the cocycle identity `τ^{n+1} ∘ d_C^n = −d_A^{n+1} ∘ τ^n`, built by
      ℕ-recursion via `Injective.factorThru` against the per-stage cosyzygy monos. The exactness
      step uses `InjectiveResolution.exact` (ker d_C^n = im d_C^{n-1}) `[expected]` and the complex
      identity `d_A∘d_A = 0`; the per-stage mono is the already-proven
      `mono_biprod_lift_factorThru_of_exact`. Model the recursion on Mathlib's
      `InjectiveResolution.ofCocomplex` / `exact_f_d` / `ofCocomplex_exactAt_succ`
      (`Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Abelian/Injective/Resolution.lean`). This may be realized via
      several helper lemmas (e.g. a base-stage `β` + an inductive `τ` lemma); the blueprint name
      above is the target. **If the recursion stalls, hand off a precise decomposition** — the
      effort-breaker has a ready 3-way re-break (augmentation / inductive lift / cocycle).
   2. **`CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact_dComp`** (`lem:horseshoe_dComp`): the
      matrix differential `[[d_A, τ], [0, d_C]]` satisfies `d ∘ d = 0` (off-diagonal cancels by
      the cocycle identity). `\uses` twist.
   3. **`CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact_chainMap`** (`lem:horseshoe_chainMap`):
      `biprod.inl`/`biprod.snd` are chain maps for `d_B`, recovering the degreewise splitting.
   4. **`CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact_resolvesMiddle`**
      (`lem:horseshoe_resolvesMiddle`): `I_B^•` with `β` is an injective resolution of `B`, via the
      homology LES (`ShortComplex.ShortExact.homology_exact₁/₂/₃`, verified present) on the
      degreewise-split SES of complexes.
   5. **Assemble `CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact`** (`lem:injective_resolution_of_ses`,
      the horseshoe) from 1–4 + the two `\mathlibok` anchors (`Injective.instBiprod`,
      `ShortComplex.Splitting.ofHasBinaryBiproduct`, both verified present): produce the
      `InjectiveResolution B` and the degreewise-split `ShortComplex (CochainComplex 𝒜 ℕ)` whose
      `.ShortExact` holds — the exact shape `rightDerivedShiftIsoOfSplitResolutionSES` consumes.
   6. **Then close the rest of P4 (go as far as possible):** specialize the already-built
      `rightDerivedShiftIsoOfSplitResolutionSES` to the object-level
      **`CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedShiftIsoOfAcyclic`** (`lem:acyclic_dimension_shift`) by
      feeding it the horseshoe SES; then build the staircase
      **`CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedIsoOfAcyclicResolution`**
      (`lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived`, TARGET 3) with `rightDerivedZeroIsoSelf` at the
      base. Verified-present infra: `InjectiveResolution.isoRightDerivedObj`,
      `rightDerivedZeroIsoSelf`, `isZero_rightDerived_obj_injective_succ`,
      `ShortComplex.ShortExact.δIso`/`homology_exact₁/₂/₃`/`δ`.
   Build axiom-clean, as far as possible (NO sorry pins). Scaffold guidance + verified Mathlib
   names: `.archon/analogies/p4-derived-les.md` and the `/- Planner strategy -/` block in the file.
   [prover-mode: mathlib-build]

Single ready lane this iter (P4). **Parallelism check (standing directive):** P4 is the only
gate-cleared, statement-sound lane. It is a single bottom-up dependency chain in one file
(twist → dComp → chainMap → resolvesMiddle → assemble → dimension-shift → staircase), not
splittable across provers. P3 and P5 remain blocked (see Deferred). This is honest serialization.

  blueprint proves the STANDARD-cover case, but `CechAcyclic.affine`'s Lean signature takes a
  general `X.OpenCover`. **DECISION (this iter): narrow the signature to a standard-cover hypothesis
  (option a) — confirmed downstream-safe: `CechAcyclic.affine` has NO current proof consumer (grep:
  only its declaration + doc comments; `cech_computes_higherDirectImage` is still a `sorry`).**
  Execute next iter as its own focused refactor: it needs a precise "standard affine cover" Lean
  type, which is non-trivial design and should not be rushed alongside the P4 decomposition. Then
  effort-break + build the localisation/contracting-homotopy infra. The blueprint-reviewer marks
  `Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage.tex` `complete: partial` solely because of this documented gap
  (`lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis` awaits a basis-comparison criterion) — no writer action can

  (proved) and P3 (`CechAcyclic.affine`) out of `CechHigherDirectImage.lean` into their own files
  (refactor subagent; keep the protected `cech_computes_higherDirectImage` signature + path frozen).
- **Coverage debt (1-to-1 hygiene, non-gating)**: four already-proven iter-004 decls in
  `AcyclicResolution.lean` still lack `\lean{}` blueprint entries
  (`isZero_homology_mapHomologicalComplex_of_isRightAcyclic`, `shortExact_of_degreewise_splitting`,
  `shortExact_map_mapHomologicalComplex_of_degreewise_splitting`, and the substantive
  `rightDerivedShiftIsoOfSplitResolutionSES`). Add their blueprint blocks next iter (the
  `rightDerivedShiftIsoOfSplitResolutionSES` one folds under `lem:acyclic_dimension_shift` as the
  resolution-level precursor). Plus 10 inherited push-pull/Čech helpers in `CechHigherDirectImage.lean`.

## Standing notes

- `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase — never add it by hand. The false
  `\leanok` on `lem:injective_resolution_of_ses` (DAG poisoning) is fixed at root this iter

  `.δ`, `.δIso` (`Mathlib/Algebra/Homology/HomologySequence.lean`); `Injective.instBiprod`,
  `ShortComplex.Splitting.ofHasBinaryBiproduct` (horseshoe anchors, verified iter-005).
- P1 done: the `conjugateEquiv_comp` mate route is INFEASIBLE (kernel `whnf` blow-up); the
  landed proof uses `rawPushPullMap`+`subst`+pentagon. (`.archon/analogies/pushpull-functoriality.md`.)
- Stacks `cohomology.tex` retrieved iter-002 → `references/stacks-cohomology.tex` (Tags 01EO
